name: ImageMagick
what_it_does: Command-line image manipulation suite (convert, composite, mogrify, identify) with a Python binding (Wand) for programmatic image processing including format conversion, resizing, compositing, and PDF rendering.
use_cases: Convert PDFs to PNG/JPEG images within agent pipelines for vision model consumption using `convert -density 150 input.pdf output.png`
Resize, crop, and reformat images produced or consumed by agents to meet downstream API size/format requirements
Composite watermarks, overlays, or annotations onto images generated by AI image models before delivery
Use `identify` to extract image metadata (dimensions, color space, format, DPI) so agents can make format-aware routing decisions
Batch-process images with `mogrify` in agent automation workflows that need to normalize a directory of files to consistent specs
not_for: Real-time image processing pipelines requiring sub-millisecond latency — ImageMagick spawns a process per operation and is not optimized for high-throughput streaming
GPU-accelerated image processing or ML-based image transformations — use PIL/Pillow, OpenCV, or torch vision for those workloads
Environments where system-level package installation is not permitted — ImageMagick requires a native binary install, not just pip
best_when: You need a battle-tested, format-universal image conversion and manipulation tool in an agent that orchestrates file-based workflows, especially PDF-to-image conversion or multi-format normalization.
avoid_when: You are in a sandboxed environment (e.g., AWS Lambda without a layer, restricted container) where installing the ImageMagick binary is not feasible, or when policy.xml security restrictions block the formats you need.

auth.notes: No authentication — ImageMagick is a local CLI tool and Python library with no network auth layer. File system permissions govern access.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Unlimited, open source (Apache 2.0 / custom ImageMagick license)
pricing.notes: Free and open source. Available via Homebrew (macOS), apt/yum (Linux), or pip install wand (Python bindings).

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: CLI exit codes and stderr output are informative but terse; Wand Python exceptions wrap MagickCore errors and are generally readable but require checking both the exception message and the wand.exceptions hierarchy.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Image conversion operations are deterministic and idempotent given the same inputs and parameters.
known_agent_gotchas: PDF conversion requires Ghostscript as a delegate; many default ImageMagick installs (especially on locked-down servers) have PDF disabled in policy.xml — agents will get a 'not authorized' error that is not obvious to diagnose
The `-density` flag for PDF rendering must be set BEFORE the input filename in the convert command, not after; placing it after produces a low-resolution output silently
ImageMagick can consume unbounded memory and CPU on malicious or pathological input images; always set -limit memory and -limit time when processing untrusted user-supplied files in agent contexts
Wand (Python binding) requires the ImageMagick shared library version to match the version Wand was built against; mismatched installs raise a cryptic WandLibraryVersionError at import time
Color profile handling (ICC, sRGB vs CMYK) can produce unexpected color shifts on format conversion; explicitly set -colorspace sRGB when converting for web/screen output

security_score_components.security_notes: ImageMagick has a history of CVEs related to processing malicious image files (ImageTragick, etc.). Always use policy.xml to restrict enabled coders and delegates, set memory/time limits, and never process untrusted files without sandboxing. Keep the binary updated.
